statement.markdown: You are given an array of integers. Create a doubly linked list from the given array and print the elements of the linked list in both forward and backward directions. The first line of input contains the number of elements in the array. The next line contains the elements of the array separated by spaces. The output should contain the elements of the linked list in the forward direction on the first line, and the elements in the backward direction on the second line.
statement.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer N, the number of elements in the array. The second line contains N integers separated by spaces.
statement.outputFormat: The first line contains the elements of the linked list in the forward direction separated by spaces. The second line contains the elements of the linked list in the backward direction separated by spaces.
statement.notes: The input array will not be empty and will contain at least one element.

constraints.description: The number of elements in the array.

examples.explanation: The linked list is created from the given array and its elements are printed in both forward and backward directions.
The linked list is created from the given array and its elements are printed in both forward and backward directions.

hints.text: Start by defining a Node class to represent each element in the doubly linked list. This class should have attributes for the node's value, the node before it (prev), and the node after it (next).

Next, create a DoublyLinkedList class that will manage the nodes. This class should have methods to add nodes to the end of the list, print the elements in forward direction, and print the elements in backward direction.
When adding nodes to the end of the list, you need to update the next and prev pointers of the new node and the last node in the list. This will ensure that the list remains doubly linked.

To print the elements in forward direction, you can start from the head of the list and traverse the next pointers until you reach the end of the list. For printing in backward direction, you can start from the tail of the list and traverse the prev pointers until you reach the head of the list.
The key to solving this problem is to understand how to maintain the doubly linked list structure by correctly updating the next and prev pointers of each node.

editorial.title: Introduction to Doubly Linked List
editorial.intuition: The problem requires creating a doubly linked list from an array of integers and then printing the elements in both forward and backward directions. The brute force approach involves creating a new node for each element in the array and linking them together, which is correct but slow due to the overhead of node creation and linking.
editorial.bruteForce: The brute force approach has a time complexity of O(n) and a space complexity of O(n) because it creates a new node for each element in the array, resulting in n nodes. However, this approach is slow due to the overhead of node creation and linking, which can be improved by using a more efficient data structure.
editorial.betterSolution: A better solution would be to use a doubly linked list implementation that allows for efficient insertion and traversal of nodes, such as the one provided in the reference solution.
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution involves creating a doubly linked list with a head node and then appending each element from the array to the list. This approach has a time complexity of O(n) and a space complexity of O(n) because it only requires a single pass through the array to create the linked list, and the space used is proportional to the number of elements in the array.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The correctness of the optimal solution can be proven by showing that each element from the array is correctly inserted into the linked list and that the linked list can be traversed in both forward and backward directions to print the elements.
editorial.dryRun: A dry run of the optimal solution involves creating a sample array of integers, creating a doubly linked list, and then appending each element from the array to the list. The resulting linked list can then be traversed in both forward and backward directions to verify that the elements are correctly inserted and printed.
commonMistakes: Not handling the case where the input array is empty
Not correctly linking the nodes together in the doubly linked list
Not properly handling the head node of the doubly linked list
Not correctly implementing the append operation for the doubly linked list
Not properly printing the elements in both forward and backward directions
